original_hypothesis.title: approve() + nonstandard allowance semantics may enable griefing via reentrancy guard interaction with hooks-driven workflows
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: Market token approve/transfer/transferFrom are nonReentrant. If an integrator contract (e.g., vault/aggregator) attempts to call approve() during a market operation that already holds the nonReentrant lock (directly or indirectly through hooks), it will revert. This can break composability and lead to systemic DoS for integrators that expect ERC20 approve to be callable in broader flows.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: Using nonReentrant on ERC20 approve is unusual and can conflict with multicall patterns and hook-based workflows. Although not a direct fund-loss vector, it can cause real-world integration failures and lock user operations when combined with hooks that call back into the market.
deployment_assumptions: Hooks implementations or integrators perform nested calls that include approve() while another nonReentrant market function is executing
Users interact via contracts that wrap market calls into multicalls/aggregated flows
original_hypothesis.config_evidence: No integration/multicall contracts provided; depends on ecosystem usage and hooks behavior.

proof_of_signal.pattern: approve() is protected by nonReentrant, uncommon for ERC20 approvals

preconditions: Caller context already has market's transient reentrancy guard set (nonReentrant entered)
Caller/integrator attempts to call approve() within that context (direct call or via hook callback)
Integration expects approve to succeed as in standard ERC20

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: reentrancy guard applied to ERC20 approval
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: blocks nested calls and reduces composability; can brick multicall flows
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No alternative approval method or EIP-2612 permit to avoid nested approve patterns

analysis_questions: Do any hooks call back into the market token approve/transfer functions?
Do known integrations (routers, wrappers) require approve inside market calls?
Is nonReentrant required for approve given it only writes allowance?
validation_requirements: Simulate hook callback that calls approve() while deposit/transfer/withdraw is executing
Integration test with common multicall/permit patterns

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: A malicious hooks contract (or misconfigured one) deliberately performs a nested approve() call during onDeposit/onTransfer to force user operations to revert, creating a market-level DoS.
prerequisites: Hooks contract can execute arbitrary external calls

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Primarily operational DoS; may block deposits/transfers for integrators
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: May require hooks changes or new market deployment

original_hypothesis.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests for nested calls into approve() under nonReentrant lock

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detect via systematic revert patterns when hooks enabled

validation_result.mechanism: nonReentrant on ERC20 approve blocks composability
validation_result.impact_summary: The nonReentrant modifier on approve() is unnecessary (only writes allowance mapping) and prevents hooks contracts from calling approve() during any market operation callback, creating potential DoS for integrators and hooks-driven workflows.
validation_result.detailed_analysis:
The hypothesis correctly identifies that WildcatMarketToken.approve() at lines 29-35 has a `nonReentrant` modifier. This modifier uses transient storage (tstore/tload) to prevent reentrant calls. Since approve() only writes to the `allowance` mapping and emits an event (via `_approve` at lines 61-64), there is no market state read or modified, meaning the reentrancy guard provides zero security benefit.

The defect manifests when any hooks callback (onDeposit, onTransfer, onQueueWithdrawal, etc.) executes during a nonReentrant-protected market function and the hooks contract attempts to call market.approve(). Since the reentrancy guard is already set by the outer market function, the nested approve() call will revert with NoReentrantCalls().

Tracing a concrete path:
1. User calls deposit(amount) → _depositUpTo(amount) which has nonReentrant
2. _setReentrancyGuard() sets tstore(_REENTRANCY_GUARD_SLOT, 1)
3. hooks.onDeposit(msg.sender, scaledAmount, state) makes external call to hooks contract
4. If hooks contract calls market.approve(spender, amount), _setReentrancyGuard() finds tload(_REENTRANCY_GUARD_SLOT) == 1 and reverts

This is a code defect because: (a) the guard is unnecessary for approve(), (b) it breaks ERC20 composability expectations, and (c) it can cause entire market operations to revert if hooks implementations attempt approve() calls.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The proof_of_signal correctly identifies WildcatMarketToken.sol lines 29-35 as containing the nonReentrant modifier on approve(). The ReentrancyGuard.sol at lines 43-48 (_setReentrancyGuard) confirms that nested nonReentrant calls will revert. The _approve function at WildcatMarketToken.sol lines 61-64 confirms that approve only writes to the allowance mapping and emits an event - no market state interaction that would justify reentrancy protection.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: nonReentrant modifier is applied to approve() which only modifies the allowance mapping. This prevents approve() from being called during any hooks callback, since all state-modifying market functions (deposit, transfer, withdraw, etc.) also hold the nonReentrant lock when invoking hooks.

The internal _approve function only writes to the allowance mapping and emits an event. No market state (scaleFactor, scaledTotalSupply, etc.) is read or modified, proving the nonReentrant guard on the external approve() provides no security benefit.

This is the mechanism that causes the revert. When a hooks callback tries to call approve() while the outer market function holds the reentrancy lock, tload returns _ENTERED (1) and the function reverts.

calling_sequence: 2. _depositUpTo has nonReentrant modifier → _setReentrancyGuard() sets tstore guard [ReentrancyGuard.sol:54-67]
3. _getUpdatedState() accrues interest, may call totalAssets() which calls asset.balanceOf() [WildcatMarketBase.sol:318-370]
4. hooks.onDeposit(msg.sender, scaledAmount, state) makes external call to hooks contract [HooksConfig.sol:onDeposit, WildcatMarket.sol:74]
5. Hooks contract receives callback and attempts market.approve(spender, amount)
6. approve() hits nonReentrant → _setReentrancyGuard() finds tload==1 → reverts NoReentrantCalls()
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The path from deposit() through hooks.onDeposit() to a nested approve() call demonstrates the issue. The same pattern applies to all other nonReentrant market functions that invoke hooks: transfer() → hooks.onTransfer(), queueWithdrawal() → hooks.onQueueWithdrawal(), executeWithdrawal() → hooks.onExecuteWithdrawal(), borrow() → hooks.onBorrow(), repay() → hooks.onRepay(), closeMarket() → hooks.onCloseMarket(), nukeFromOrbit() → hooks.onNukeFromOrbit(). In all cases, if the hooks contract calls approve(), the transaction reverts.

deployment_assumptions_checked: Hooks implementations or integrators perform nested calls that include approve() while another nonReentrant market function is executing
Users interact via contracts that wrap market calls into multicalls/aggregated flows
config_validation.deployment_evidence: No deployment scripts provided that configure hooks implementations. The deployment scripts (LibDeployment.sol) are generic deployment utilities and do not configure specific hooks contracts or integrator patterns.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: A malicious or poorly designed hooks contract could deliberately call market.approve() during any hook callback (onDeposit, onTransfer, etc.) to cause the entire operation to revert. Since hooks are configured at market deployment time and are immutable per market, a borrower could deploy a market with hooks that call approve() in callbacks, creating a permanent DoS for specific operations. Even benign hooks that need approval functionality during callbacks would trigger this. The defect exists in the code regardless of whether specific hooks exploit it.
validation_result.impact_assessment: Medium severity. The nonReentrant on approve() is a genuine code defect that (1) provides no security benefit since approve() only modifies the allowance mapping, (2) breaks ERC20 composability expectations, and (3) creates a potential DoS vector through hooks callbacks. While not directly exploitable for fund theft, it can permanently brick market operations if hooks implementations call approve(), and it prevents legitimate composability patterns. Recovery would require deploying a new market with different hooks.
recommended_mitigations: Remove the nonReentrant modifier from approve() since it only writes to the allowance mapping and makes no external calls or state reads that could be exploited through reentrancy
If some form of protection is desired, use nonReentrantView (read-only check) instead, though even that is unnecessary
Consider implementing EIP-2612 permit() as an alternative approval mechanism that doesn't require a separate transaction and avoids the reentrancy issue entirely
Add integration tests that verify approve() can be called from within hooks callbacks
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy a market with a custom hooks contract that has onDeposit enabled. 2. In the hooks contract's onDeposit callback, call market.approve(someAddress, someAmount). 3. Call market.deposit(amount) from an EOA. 4. Observe that the transaction reverts with NoReentrantCalls(). 5. Repeat for other hook callbacks (onTransfer, onQueueWithdrawal, etc.) to confirm the issue persists across all nonReentrant paths.
uncertainties: Whether any planned or existing hooks implementations actually attempt to call approve() during callbacks - this determines practical exploitability
Whether the nonReentrant on approve() was an intentional design decision or an oversight from applying nonReentrant uniformly to all external state-changing functions
Whether the SphereX guard (sphereXGuardExternal) would independently block the nested call even without nonReentrant
additional_testing_needed: Test approve() callability from within each hooks callback type (onDeposit, onTransfer, onQueueWithdrawal, onExecuteWithdrawal, onBorrow, onRepay, onCloseMarket, onNukeFromOrbit)
Test approve() callability from ERC777 tokensReceived callback if the underlying asset supports ERC777
Test multicall patterns that combine approve() with other market operations in a single transaction
Verify that removing nonReentrant from approve() introduces no security regression by attempting allowance manipulation attacks
